for $30 million, which struck me as a bit crazy.  Rembrandt prints, which are more or less the same
thing over time, they’ve gone up by two times  or three times over a few years. What’s going
on here? Old Masters coming back because people  are sick of the rest, or is it just anomalous?
GRAHAM-DIXON: It might be a little bit anomalous.  I’ve been following it because I was friends with
a guy at Sotheby’s who did a lot of research into  a recently discovered Rembrandt. It felt like
there’s a ceiling for a Rembrandt painting.  This is a lovely painting of the Nativity,
a monochrome painting. It sold for £10 million or  $10 million, which is the same price as the last
two Rembrandt self-portraits have sold for. When  you think an Andy Warhol, which is essentially a
screen print, is selling for maybe $100 million,  it does seem really weird and underpriced.
Yet, as you say, the prints have been selling  for figures that, compared to the paintings,
are really high. I don’t know. It is my belief  that Rembrandt is a painter just full of love
for the world. The values that are enshrined in  his paintings are important values. Maybe that
we live in such tough times, maybe people just  feel the value of those things all the more.
COWEN: There are attributions for both Vermeer  and Rembrandt, as you well know. How uncertain
are they? What does the uncertainty hinge upon? GRAHAM-DIXON: I’m not interested in paintings
that weren’t in the house much. I know what  they were, so I don’t need to get involved
with attribution. I’m only really interested in  the paintings that were in the house. You have
one here in Washington. COWEN: Sure.
GRAHAM-DIXON: Woman Holding a Balance. COWEN: It’s now accepted. Formerly,
it was uncertain. I’m never sure  whether I should trust the new judgment.
GRAHAM-DIXON: Well, there’s no question. As I show  in my book, one of the things that emerges from
the book, once you actually know why the paintings  were made, once you know the beliefs of the people
for whom they were painted, suddenly, one of  the things that emerges really clearly about
the paintings is that a lot of them were painted  in twos. The Woman Holding a Balance is clearly,
without any doubt, the pair to the famous painting  in the Rijksmuseum called The Milkmaid. All of the
painting’s titles are nothing to do with  Vermeer. They’re all modern inventions.
She’s not a milkmaid, and she’s not just a woman  with a balance. What the two pictures represent
is the two aspects. They simplify Christianity to  its bare essentials: Do well, think well. At the
end of the day, you consider, have I behaved as  Jesus would have wanted me to behave today? Have
I done everything that I should have? I ask my  conscience this question. That’s what the Woman
Holding a Balance is doing. She’s not weighing  gold. She’s weighing her conscience. Behind her,
there’s a painting of the last judgment where  the souls are being weighed by Archangel Michael.
She has weighed her own soul. There’s nothing  in the balance. The scales are empty. They’re
perfectly poised. Her conscience is clean. The  other lady, exactly the same scale to the room
she occupies, the same light source coming from  the left, she is crumbling bread into a bowl of
milk. She’s making what the Dutch call bread  pudding, which was the food that you gave when
you administered charity to the old and to the  very young, people who were too infirm or old
to be able to chew solid food. You gave them this  pap, this bread pudding. That’s what she’s doing.
She’s robed in blue, almost as if she were  a modern Madonna. If you’re seeing them in
traditional Christian terms, they are Martha  and Mary. They are the contemplative life and
the active life. If there’s any doubt in your mind  about them being a pair, which they’ve never been
identified as a pair as far as I’m aware up until  now. They were certainly not hung as a pair in the
great Rijksmuseum exhibition that happened a few  years ago. If you look at the two pictures, if you
get them up onto a computer screen together,  you’ll see that there’s a nail in the wall.
Just above and to the left of each woman in  exactly the same place. It’s Vermeer tapping
us on the shoulder from 400 years ago and  saying, “Yes, if there’s any doubt that these
paintings are a pair, look at the nail.” COWEN: What did you learn from the great
Rijksmuseum exhibit of more or less all the  Vermeers? When you saw them all together,
it must have been the first time  for you. What new idea did you have?
GRAHAM-DIXON: Well, I was testing the big  new idea, which is the book. It was great
timing for me because I had completed the  first draft of my manuscript, or most of it,
certainly when it came to the paintings.  I’d written more or less everything that
I wanted to write about the paintings. There  was this fantastic opportunity to see them all.
The Rijksmuseum’s director, Taco Dibbits, who I’d  known for a long time, was kind enough to give me,
in effect, a free pass because it was  a very tough ticket. I think I went
to the exhibition maybe 30 or 40 times. I went many, many times just to really
embed in the paintings. You had to go  toward the end of the day, particularly
when the crowds slackened off a bit, or right  at the beginning if you got in before anyone
else. You’d get a little bit of time, especially  with the paintings at the far end of the show.
It was great. I’m very grateful to them. COWEN: Putting aside the details of your
own thesis, what is it you think you see  more of on the 17th visit as compared,
say, to the second visit? Most are all  of the pictures you had seen before,
anyway, in other places, just not together. GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes. I think the subtlety of
the light, things like that, and certain details.  Those nails in the wall may have come fairly late,
actually. I think I may have seen those late,  although the paintings were actually hung 120
yards apart, because when they put on that  exhibition, they obviously weren’t aware of
the context that I’d discovered. No one could  really be in a position to understand those
sorts of meanings, perhaps. It’s only with the  discovery of the context that it’s possible to
see what he was actually trying to do. It was a  very interesting exhibition from the point of view
of looking at the pictures purely as paintings  because there was no strong narrative drive to it.
COWEN: What do you think of the  quality of the restorations?
GRAHAM-DIXON: Vermeer’s paintings were in  fantastically good condition. I was lucky
enough 28 years ago to film at the Mauritshuis  with the BBC crew just at the point where the
View of Delft had been restored, and the Girl  with a Pearl Earring had been restored. I could
see very clearly, and he talked me through  it. It was a man called Jørgen Wadum who’d
done the restoration. The minimal retouching  necessary in a Vermeer, almost nothing wrong
with the painting. There’s no controversial  filling in of void space, which you normally have,
certainly with a Renaissance painting. To what extent do you bring that area
of loss back? How much do you harmonize  it so that it doesn’t disturb the modern
eye looking at the painting? You can’t really  leave a gaping hole. In the case of Vermeer,
there aren’t these gaping holes. One thing I  remember that was fantastic, which is one in
the eye for the people who are always going  on about the camera obscura, which I think is
one of the more boring things people talk about  when they talk about Vermeer. Did he use a camera
obscura and all that? If you look closely, as  Jørg showed me, at the highlight in the pearl, in
the Girl with a Pearl Earring, there’s a splash.  He’s dropped the paint. He’s dropped the paint.
Likewise, if you look at The Lacemaker, the  painting in the Louvre, which I controversially
describe as a painting about maternal love, and  I say that the sewing cushion with all its red
threads is a visual metaphor placed there by  Vermeer for the woman’s placenta, nourishing
the baby. She’s making a piece of lace for her  baby, and she’s pregnant, and the sewing cushion.
All of those red threads, they’ve actually been  thrown. It’s like a little Vermeer Jackson Pollock
prefiguration. He’s thrown the paint. COWEN: What makes George
Stubbs such an underrated painter? GRAHAM-DIXON: I think he’s been underrated
forever because of his subject matter. I blame the  French. The French in the 17th century invented a
system for ranking works of art by their subject  matter. Up at the top, you and I, we could put on
some armor and confront each other with swords,  and Poussin would paint us. That would be a
history painting done from the life. That would  be at the pinnacle. Then would be a painting of
an event, maybe a meeting between great men.  Then, below that, there would be a portrait.
You’d keep going down, and eventually you’d get to  paintings of still lifes, like flowers and fruit,
or paintings of animals. These were the  lowest works of art because they featured
the basest things. I think that Stubbs, to  a certain extent, was the victim of that.
COWEN: Are we now able to see them properly? GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes. It’s been no problem since
Stubbs and Constable, more than anyone  else, slightly controversial to say,
but I think they paved the way for modernism  because they showed, not necessarily deliberately,
but they showed that anything could be painted,  anything at all, like a piece of mud or a piece
of a river could be painted in such a way as to  touch on the very highest thoughts and ideas and
beliefs and feelings that subject matter is  completely, in a sense, irrelevant. Cézanne
picked up on that when he said, “I want to stun  Paris with an apple. I’ll paint an apple, and I’ll
paint it with such astonishing concentration  that you’ll see it as an epistemological
challenge to all of your philosophy.” I think that French art gets that from
British art because British art has to be like  that because that’s all the aristocrats are going
to commission. They look down on people like  Stubbs, in a sense. If I’m an aristocrat in
the 18th century and I want a really important  picture, I’ll go to Italy and I’ll buy one,
thank you very much, and I’ll buy my history  painting from Titian. You, sir, Stubbs, you just
paint my horse. It’s my racehorse, and I’m very  fond of him. Paint him well and don’t scare him.
COWEN: You think the French also have underrated  Géricault, who painted and drew many horses?
GRAHAM-DIXON: Géricault, of course, went to  England. Géricault spent some of his formative
years in the early 19th century in London. He  brought some of that spirit of English revolution
to French art. Interestingly, Géricault’s great  friend and the man who looked up to Géricault more
than anyone else, Delacroix, who did he idolize  out of all the British artists? Constable. He
loved Constable, Delacroix. Somebody should write  a book about it. This special relationship that’s
partly to do with intense jealousy and hatred,  but also to do with unexpected mutual admiration.
COWEN: How would you contrast what you absorb  from a Géricault horse compared to a Stubbs horse?
GRAHAM-DIXON: Stubbs, I think, he’s not painting  himself. Géricault’s painting himself. Géricault
horses are self-portraits. Stubbs is  trying to understand how the world works,
and Stubbs understands the miracle. He really  sees it as a miracle. It’s very interesting.
In Stubbs’s later engravings, he seems to work  his way toward the beginnings of the Darwinian
theory of evolution because he’s done a lot of  dissection, and he’s beginning to see that there
are similarities in the way that a chicken’s hips  are formed to the way that human hips are formed.
He’s beginning to wonder whether there might  not be some contiguity of relationship between
the different animal species. It’s very  near the end of his life, so of course,
he never pushes forward to it. I believe it’s  not entirely eccentric to see the currents of
thought that led Stubbs to those ideas as being  contributory to the great river of Darwinian
theory that’s going to come about 50 years later.  I think he’s confronting that mystery of existence
and of species and who we are, and maybe we can  understand who we are through looking at a horse.
I also sometimes get from his horses, I  get a tremendous sense of species fellow
feeling. There’s that great painting that’s  in Mount Stewart. I can’t remember the name
of the racehorse, Hambletonian. The racehorse  had been flogged to within an inch of its life
to win this great prize for the owner. Stubbs  has painted the horse at the moment after
the race when the groom is comforting the  horse and the horse is flecked with blood,
and its eyes are wild. Stubbs paints the picture  almost as if he’s painting a concentration camp
victim or something. He’s saying, “How could  you do this to this wonderful creature, this
wonderful being?” I love Stubbs. You can tell. COWEN: Many are held in the state of Virginia,
as you probably know, in the Mellon Collection  in Richmond. I’ve seen them many times.
GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes, that’s Basil Taylor. The  great Stubbs expert was huge friends with Paul
Mellon. It’s all about who you know and when  you know them. Paul Mellon has got more money
than anyone else. He loves art more than anyone  else, and he becomes friends with Basil Taylor,
which is, I suppose, a shame for England, but a  wonderful thing for Yale. My favorite picture,
I think, in the whole Mellon, is that picture  of the zebra. Do you know that picture?
COWEN: I think I do. GRAHAM-DIXON: I love that picture.
COWEN: Another favorite book of yours of mine, the  Howard Hodgkin study. Now, it seemed to me in the
1990s, there was some chance Hodgkin would have a  big breakthrough. The New York Times wrote him up.
He might become this major contemporary painter,  but it never quite happened. His prices are still
pretty reasonable. The works are lovely. Who was  right, the people who thought he was incredibly
important, or that he’s a minor modernist? GRAHAM-DIXON: I wouldn’t call time on anybody’s
reputation. Hey, Vermeer’s a pretty good case.  Vermeer had no reputation at all for 400 years.
I think Howard’s doing okay. COWEN: I would argue for him.
What’s your view now, because it’s  decades since you wrote the book?
GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes. No, that’s fair enough. I see  the patterns by which the world values things.
I don’t pay a great deal of attention to them  unless I’m asked a question like that. I think,
“Oh, gosh. Of course, this is what people  think about,” and I don’t think about it.
I just think about what they are. I think they’re  such wonderful pictures. Eventually, they will be
just as expensive as the pictures of Matisse  or Vuillard or Bonnard or those other artists.
Howard, I think, belongs within that tradition  very much. He’s an unusual painter because he
decides to extend and deepen a tradition that he  has nothing to do with personally, nationally,
or historically. He just becomes so fascinated  by it. I’d probably add Degas to that, too.
COWEN: Indian miniatures,  right, which he collected.
GRAHAM-DIXON: He always said they  weren’t an influence on his work.
COWEN: I never believed that. GRAHAM-DIXON: I never did either,
to be honest. You’re darn right. COWEN: He had incredible
taste in Indian miniatures. GRAHAM-DIXON: He did, he did. What a wonderful
collection he had of Indian miniatures. I used to  love going to see Indian things with him. We used
to go because he used to live right behind the  British Museum. When I was a young art critic,
I became friends with him, and I used to go often  to have lunch with him. We would always go in to
look at the Indian things. COWEN: What was he like?
GRAHAM-DIXON: He would be very emotional. Very  emotional. I would say, “Oh, I loved that painting
that you made of the Henry Moore at the Bottom  of the Garden.” Then he would suddenly remember,
because every painting that he did was like  a moment in his life. It had its origins in a
particular moment. There were emotions brought  back in tranquility, if you like. He would
suddenly remember what was happening on that day  and how much he loved that person, but they’re
dead now. It was just someone to turn the taps on.  He would weep great big tears, proper big tears,
like Toad of Toad Hall tears, except they weren’t  fake tears; they were real tears. Very emotional.
You had to rather watch your step. You never knew  if you’d have to take a timeout for 20 minutes.
COWEN: Do you feel there’s a category of artist  where it’s now impossible to still love them? If I
think, say, of Malevich, who is a great artist and  very important historically, but when I see them,
I’m left cold. Maybe I’ve seen them too many  times, or maybe there are too many fakes,
or maybe that kind of image has been too  commercialized, but it now does nothing for me,
and I’m not sure that will change, but  without taking any credit away from
him. Is that a meaningful concept to you? GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes. I don’t feel like that,
but I don’t look at Malevich all the time. I’ve  become more and more interested in context. When
I look at Malevich, I just see the Black Square,  and I just see that moment. So many millions of
people have died. This is a revolution. This  is a moment when the people will rise up,
and all these beautiful ideas are in the air, and  all these people dreaming their dreams, and that’s
going to be crushed. If I see those pictures  and I’m thinking those thoughts, I can still
find that I look at those pictures and I might  feel like a choking-up feeling because of that.
I feel that deeply with Vermeer. It’s so tragic.  If I look at the View of Delft, the Collegiants
and the Remonstrants, Vermeer’s people, they  believed that as people read more books, as
people became more educated, they would all read  Socrates, they would all read good philosophy,
they would read the Bible. They would become  friends. Everyone would become friends with
everyone else. All religious discord would  disappear. War would become a thing of the past.
Tyrants would hand over their power to the people. They genuinely believed that, and they
didn’t just believe it. It was a belief in  the New Jerusalem, which was very widespread
among the Jewish communities of that period, as  well as the Catholics, the Protestants. Lots of
people believed in this, but only the Collegiants  believed that it would happen, not by some Messiah
coming down with lightning and thunder. They  actually believed that the spirit of Christ, by
which they meant the spirit of love and the spirit  of peace, would inhabit people automatically. The
more they could read and understand and talk  to one another in these Collegiant meetings,
that peace on earth would happen, and that it  wouldn’t be some kind of amazing miracle thing.
That heaven on earth looks like your hometown  on a really sunny, clear day. The women are at
the shops. The children are playing. The boats  are ready to take you to wherever you want to
go. That is heaven on earth, and that’s what that  painting is of. Every time I see that painting,
in a different way from the Malevich,  it’s in touching distance. We could
have that. We could have that more or less  everywhere if we just stop being so stupid.
COWEN: Past Vermeer and Rembrandt, is Fabritius  the next greatest Dutch artist from that era,
or who comes close to those two? GRAHAM-DIXON: Well, “Fabritius,”
as I’ve been taught to pronounce him. COWEN: Forgive my American pronunciation for all
these names, but it’s what people understand here. GRAHAM-DIXON: I always pronounce him like that.
The only reason is because I did my book as an  audiobook recording, so I had a lot of tuition
in pronouncing names right. I think Fabritius is a  fantastic painter. He’s definitely, in my opinion,
the source for Vermeer’s wonderful technique.  This tonal painting that no one else does.
Fabritius does it. He’s in Rembrandt’s studio.  They know Leonardo da Vinci’s theories because
they’ve just been published in France. The  book’s in Rembrandt’s studio. We know that
they know these ideas, and he’s doing it. He’s  putting it into practice. Without Fabritius,
Vermeer couldn’t paint the way he does. I  think the tragedy of Fabritius, and again—
COWEN: He dies young. GRAHAM-DIXON: —he dies young, but people
don’t really understand. How does Fabritius die?  You probably know how he dies. He dies because
Delft is the largest weapons store in Europe.  Somebody goes in with a candle, and it blows up,
and a third of the whole city is destroyed.  Fabritius is blown to pieces. Again, why would
that happen? It’s because they’re still at war.  This is a culture completely at war. There’s war
everywhere. I’m going later, so maybe I’m wrong,  but I’m going to go to the National Gallery of
Art in Washington, to the Dutch section, and I’m  going to see, is there any mention made anywhere,
in any room, of the fact that half the  population of Germany died during this period?
COWEN: I don’t think so. GRAHAM-DIXON: That 80 years of war
between Holland and Spain had led to the massacre  of huge numbers of civilian populations. If you
don’t understand these things, how on earth—when  you see a radiant painting by Vermeer of peace,
it’s not just a painting of a woman in a room.  A painting of a woman writing, itself it’s like,
“Gosh, she’s not being assaulted. She’s not  being grabbed out of her home. She’s not
having her possessions taken away,” because all  over Europe, that was what was happening. These
ordinary scenes are actually charged with meaning  that we can’t recover unless we know the context.
That’s why I write books, really, is to try  and understand. If you don’t understand what
they were thinking about, how on earth can  you understand what’s in these pictures?
COWEN: Would the Dutch painters  have seen van Eyck’s and other
Low Country paintings? The best ones. GRAHAM-DIXON: I don’t know what paintings Vermeer
would have seen, but I’m sure that he had seen  paintings by van Eyck. It’s quite hard to piece
together what they would have seen. There were  dealers in Amsterdam dealing in really wonderful
paintings of that school. Flemish art is around  because, after all, the Low Countries is the
Low Countries. Yes, it’s been divided into North  and South. I think he would be familiar with that
tradition. For example, there’s an early painting  in the Mauritshuis of Diana and her companions.
I think there’s this very strong Christian  undertow to this classical painting where
you’ve got a basin and a cloth, and one  of Diana’s nymphs is washing her feet,
much as Mary Magdalene washes the feet of Christ  in Flemish altarpieces. It looks so much like
the paraphernalia that you’d see in a Flemish  altarpiece that Vermeer must have seen one
of those altarpieces to borrow that from it. COWEN: When you think about contemporary art,
which has become quite controversial  in the United States, how healthy is
the current state of affairs in general? GRAHAM-DIXON: I cannot even begin to pretend
to do my exam answer because— COWEN: No, you’ve been a judge
for the Turner Prize. GRAHAM-DIXON: I have.
COWEN: You see plenty in London.  You get around the world.
GRAHAM-DIXON: I do, but to be perfectly honest,  I’ve spent seven years only looking at archives
and thinking about Dutch art, really. I’m  currently thinking about a Renaissance artist,
but I have hardly seen any modern art at all.  What I have difficulty with in contemporary art
is the very elevated reputations certain artists  have. Because the modern art world seems to be
so institutionally rigid. If Museum X has lots  of paintings by Richter, Museum Y also does,
Museum A, B, C, D, E, they’ve all got lots  of paintings by Richter. In my opinion,
Richter’s work of the last 20 years is almost  worthless. I think it’s just not interesting.
I think it’s coasting. I might even go so far  as to say it’s almost like printing money.
You make these pictures. They’re very mechanically  made. They sell for $20 million each. How,
within the existing institutions of the art world,  is that downgrade going to happen? I can say
that, but it’s not going to make any difference.  The museums aren’t going to change. Like my book
on Vermeer. If people take my book on Vermeer  seriously, it means that every caption by every
painting on every website of every museum will  have to be completely different. Now, I personally
think that in 20 years’ time they will be, but I  know it’s not going to happen this week or next.
How’s it going to happen with these other artists? COWEN: Take someone like de Kooning, who’s a truly
great artist, but as you’ve pointed out yourself,  the variance of the works is very high. There’s a
lot of bad or only so-so de Koonings. Are those  still due for a spill in terms of esteem, price,
display, or do you just think the way it’s at,  well, he’s de Kooning, we’ll grant him that,
and the good ones are good enough? GRAHAM-DIXON: I think it’s an interesting
question. Again, I remember I reviewed in New  York many years ago—I don’t know when it would be,
shortly before de Kooning died—there  was an exhibition of his late work,
and people were quite skeptical about it.  I thought, looking at it, there was so much
of what had been in the earlier work. COWEN: I think it’s often quite good,
some of them. GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes.
I felt that he was talking to himself. When I  feel that an artist is talking to themselves,
when it’s real, this is what I love about Vermeer,  that it’s so real. He’s only painting what he
cares about. That’s it. He wants to paint the  conscience. He’s not thinking about who likes it,
who’s going to like it, how much it’s going to  be worth. Likewise, de Kooning, at the end of
his life, he—not likewise, but in a very different  way, but he’s within a different tradition and so
on. He’s genuinely thinking about what he’s doing. I’ve seen artists, an artist that shall not be
named. I remember being at the Royal Academy  and standing behind this artist in the queue
for the cloakroom. There was a very pretty  attendant taking coats, and he just said,
“Do you not know who I am?” She said no. He said,  “I am a very important artist.” I thought, “Oh,
man. It’s over.” As soon as that happens, you  know it’s over. You have to be thinking about
the art that you’re making, and you have to be  caring about it. I think we can always tell when
that’s the case, or I hope we can. I think we can. COWEN: When you’re acting in a role as an art
buyer, obviously, you ask yourself, “Do I like  the work? Can I afford the work?” What’s a deeper
question that you would apply that other art  buyers should apply for making better decisions?
GRAHAM-DIXON: I don’t think I’ve ever bought  art. I was on the Government Art Committee,
which buys art for embassies, for the  government’s art collection. That’s a very
specific kind of buying because you’re buying art  by British artists that will favorably represent
British art abroad in embassies and so on.  I don’t know. If I were trying to advise,
you mean advise like a collector or someone? COWEN: Or yourself. You could afford a Rembrandt
print. Not the very best ones perhaps. GRAHAM-DIXON: A fifth state, yes. No,
you’re quite right. I think I bought a John  Nash for about £20 the other day in a junk shop.
COWEN: A lot of Bloomsbury works are quite  affordable. I think they’re underpriced.
GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes. I’ve got a friend who is a  very rich fund manager, and he only collects work
by female artists only. That’s all he buys. COWEN: Yes, they’re also underpriced still.
GRAHAM-DIXON: He’s rediscovered a number  of women artists of the prewar period in
Britain. He tells me that they’re so  unknown that once he’s discovered one,
he can then find their work in other sales, but  it’s often not listed as being by them because no
one else really knows about this person. He’s  saying he’s put together 25 works by Phoebe,
whoever she is, who was an RA in the 1920s, and  he said, “I’m buying these things for £5 each.”
He said it’s a great myth that the museums  of the world are correcting the patriarchal
imbalances of the past by buying female— COWEN: I agree.
GRAHAM-DIXON: He said it’s not actually happening.  There’s lots of fantastic work by women artists,
and museums aren’t buying any of  it. They could buy it for £5 if
they actually just got their fingers out. COWEN: Textiles are grossly underpriced,
I think, relative to aesthetic quality. GRAHAM-DIXON: I agree. Actually, funnily enough,
that’s a very good point. One of the very  few things I have bought in the last 10 years
was a Mennonite textile made somewhere in the  southern states of America out of little bits
of the black suits of the mourning costumes  of the men. A beautiful thing, and it was,
I think, $1,000. Goodness knows how long it  took to make. It’s a beautiful thing. It’s
like an abstract work of art. Absolutely.  No, I agree. That’s the thing with art,
once you find a corner that you like—that’s why  I have to not get too involved. Because if I did—
COWEN: You’d do nothing else. GRAHAM-DIXON: —I’d end up
buying 100 Mennonite quilts. COWEN: Obviously, you live in
Britain. If you think about the physical  environment of Britain, there are so many
wonderful towns. Bath is famous. Somewhere like  Bradford can be stunning, not every part of it.
If I think about more recent neighborhoods,  I understand King Charles may be trying,
but it’s hard for me to think of a single place in  America also that I consider highly attractive in
the same way that British-built neighborhoods  might have been knockouts, say before World
War II, whatever the cutoff date may be. Why has that happened? Why do we not build
beautiful neighborhoods anymore?  Maybe you challenge the premise,
but where is it really? You could say  maybe Copenhagen, maybe parts of Helsinki,
but the Western world seems to be failing at that. GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes. Generally speaking, we appear
to be living through a crisis of confidence in  our own ability to make the world a better place.
We talk about it a lot. We agonize over things  like the environment and issues like that,
but there’s very little of that. I made a TV  series years ago about the art of Scandinavia.
There was that amazing project in Scandinavia  where they decided they were just going to build
5 million houses, and they did it. They were  beautiful houses. They were made for ordinary
people to live in. Even actually in America in the  1950s, there were not dissimilar projects to that,
utopian schemes of that kind, which  produced very beautiful neighborhoods.
COWEN: It’s now a long time ago, isn’t it? GRAHAM-DIXON: It’s a long time ago. And I
keep coming back to this book about Vermeer, but  that’s what I find so touching about those people.
There’s a painting called The Little Street by  Vermeer, which is on the cover of the book. It
shows us the house in which Vermeer’s paintings  all hung. I have discovered it’s actually the
house of his patrons. If you could press way  face to the window, you’d see Vermeer’s paintings
inside that house. That painting is about what  it is that makes a beautiful neighborhood,
and it’s belief and it’s cooperation, it’s  toleration, it’s love, it’s all the things.
Vermeer’s Remonstrant preacher friend of the  family wrote a book, and in his book he talks
about religion and faith, and he says it’s like a  house. Everyone has to put a brick in. It’s not a
house one person makes and everyone else worships  in. Everyone has to put a brick in. That painting,
if you look at the join between the two houses,  the house which used to hold all Vermeer’s
paintings and the house next door, each owner  of each house has mended a bit of it. You’ve got
this sense that in this neighborhood, everyone  has contributed a bit to each other’s houses.
James Fenton wrote a great poem about Jerusalem,  which is essentially a poem written a number of
years ago about the Israeli-Palestine conflict.  It’s got these two terrible crushing last lines:
“I have destroyed your home, you have destroyed my  home.” Vermeer’s painting is the opposite of that:
“I have made your home, you have made my home.  I have repaired your home.” You can imagine that
painting opening out into the whole of a beautiful  town, and in Delft you can still visit that town.
The more I’ve thought about  this book I’ve been writing,
the more I’ve felt I’ve been writing almost  meditations on the lessons that those people,
Vermeer and his friends, had to teach us,  because you can’t make anything beautiful,
you can’t make a good community unless you have  beliefs. To me, people have just lost their
beliefs, and they don’t believe strongly  enough in the importance of cooperation,
and it’s sad. It’s not that it can’t be got  back. I don’t believe it can’t be got back.
What you touch on, it’s a raw nerve. COWEN: Two final questions. First,
let’s say you meet a young British person who  wants to be some version of the next Andrew
Graham-Dixon. What non-obvious advice do you  have for them? Work hard, look at art, sure,
but what do they really need to hear from you? GRAHAM-DIXON: My first advice is never, ever,
ever try to be a version of someone else. COWEN: They want to be in the world of art
and do TV documentaries and YouTube  and write books. That’s what I mean.
GRAHAM-DIXON: Yes, I know. I think build networks  of friends and don’t rely on corporations. Do it
yourself, that would be my advice. I was lucky  because I was a young would-be art critic and
all the jobs were taken. There were six national  newspapers and they all had art critics. They were
all over 60 and they’d been in their jobs for  20 years. I was lucky because a new newspaper
was founded called The Independent, and they  hired me to be the art critic. That’s not
going to happen for most people. The BBC employed me to make art
documentaries. The BBC doesn’t do that anymore.  They’re not interested in culture anymore,
which is weird, but they’re not. Nowadays,  you’d have to do it for yourself—self-fund,
self-finance, and above all, have fun. COWEN: Just to plug your book again, Andrew
Graham-Dixon, Vermeer: A Life Lost and Found. I  like this very much, as I do all of Andrew’s other
books. Last question, what will you do next? GRAHAM-DIXON: I’ve found a really interesting
painting by one of the greats,  and I’m writing a book about it.
COWEN: That’s a secret, that painting? GRAHAM-DIXON: It is a secret.
COWEN: Great. GRAHAM-DIXON: It is a secret.
COWEN: I look forward to the book. Andrew,
thank you very much. GRAHAM-DIXON: Cheers, Tyler.
